metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify what the `--force` guard test is actually pinning: if the claim-gate path (`plan_review_precheck` / `claim_gate_check`) makes NO LLM call on any path, document this explicitly in the test (the guard is 'no LLM call exists here at all', not 'force skips it') — or redirect the guard test to a gate that genuinely has an LLM call that the bypass skips (e.g. the completion-verification close gate with `--force-close`).
findings.finding: The plan's --force guard-test premise is wrong: it asserts '--force causes the gate to SKIP the gate LLM call entirely' and that the injected FunctionModel is 'NEVER invoked' as a meaningful invariant to preserve. But plan_review_precheck (the --force claim-gate path) makes NO LLM call on any code path — claim_gate_check is a local HMAC verify with 'NO LLM and NO network' (gates.py:84-151, confirmed by file read). The FunctionModel is never invoked regardless of whether --force is used, making the guard test trivially pass for the wrong reason. If the gate later gains an LLM call, the guard test still passes (trivially) and silently fails to pin the invariant it describes.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Guard tests' paragraph; specifically '--force (the claim bypass flag)' guard test description
findings.suggested_fix: In the guard test, assert the trivially-correct claim explicitly (e.g. 'plan_review_precheck has no LLM call; FunctionModel injected but never reached') and add a comment that the real skip-invariant is on the --force-close path (completion close gate), where the gate IS enabled AND the bypass at transition_close.py:114-115 short-circuits before the billable verifier runs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the per-criterion two-scope scenario description: the current per-chunk fail-open behavior is silent findings-drop + `coverage['chunk_errors']` increment (pass1.py:458), NOT an INDETERMINATE-marked criterion — update the Scope section to describe the correct observable so the test implementer asserts the right behavior.
findings.finding: The plan's two-scope per-criterion scenario description says 'one flaky finder marks its criterion INDETERMINATE and the review continues,' but the actual codebase seam (`pass1.py:458`) is `except Exception: coverage['chunk_errors'] += 1; logger.warning(...)` — it DROPS the failing chunk's findings silently without aborting, and does NOT write any INDETERMINATE marker. The INDETERMINATE path is reserved for propagated `LLMUnavailableError` (systemic). The plan thus points the test at a behavior that does not exist in today's code.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Two-scope scenarios: systemic (whole op) vs per-criterion (one unit among N; the plan-review pass-1 fail-open, where one flaky finder marks its criterion INDETERMINATE and the review continues)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'one flaky finder marks its criterion INDETERMINATE and the review continues' with 'one failing chunk's findings are silently dropped (coverage["chunk_errors"] is incremented) and the review continues with the remaining chunks' findings — assertable via the returned coverage dict.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the per-criterion injection mechanism: define what exception type the FunctionModel raises for a single flaky finder (must be non-LLMUnavailableError, e.g. `RuntimeError` or `StructuredOutputError`) and how the FunctionModel is scoped to fail only one invocation (e.g. a call-counter that raises on the N-th call, or separate runner instances for 'systemic runner' vs 'per-criterion failing runner').
findings.finding: The per-criterion (one-unit-among-N) scope injection mechanism is unspecified: the plan says 'one flaky finder marks its criterion INDETERMINATE and the review continues' but never defines how to inject a non-systemic failure for exactly one FunctionModel invocation while letting the rest succeed. pass1.py:453-460 shows LLMUnavailableError is re-raised systemically while any other Exception is swallowed per-chunk — the test must inject a non-LLMUnavailableError from only the N-th runner call, but neither call-count dispatch in FunctionModel nor a per-criterion runner-switch mechanism is described anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Scope section, 'Two-scope scenarios: systemic (whole op) vs per-criterion'; plan_review/pass1.py:451-460
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'For the per-criterion scope case, inject a FunctionModel that raises `RuntimeError("simulated per-criterion failure")` on only the first call (using a call-counter), passed as `model_override` to a `PydanticAIRunner` used for ONE criterion's invocation; the remaining criteria use a normal canned-response FunctionModel. This exercises `pass1.py:458-460` (the `except Exception` swallow path) without triggering `pass1.py:453-457` (the `LLMUnavailableError` re-raise path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `content_filter` finish_reason case must use `mode='structured'` (or `mode='findings'`) so the injection reaches `_pai_structured` and exercises `structured.check_stop_reason` at `runner.py:483` — add an explicit note in the Scope bullet that the canned-finish_reason test uses structured mode, since text mode never calls `check_stop_reason`.
findings.finding: The `content_filter` finish_reason case must use `mode='structured'` or `mode='findings'` (the PromptedOutput branch) to reach `structured.check_stop_reason` at runner.py:483 — that call is absent from the `text` mode path (runner.py:325-329). The plan does not specify which mode to use for this case, leaving the implementer free to choose `text` mode, which silently bypasses the only place `check_stop_reason` is called and makes the test assert no real behavior.
findings.location: Scope section, 'content_filter finish_reason case' bullet; runner.py:325-329 vs runner.py:477-490
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, amend the finish_reason bullet: 'the model returns a CANNED ModelResponse(finish_reason="content_filter") via mode="structured" (or mode="findings") — this RAISES NO EXCEPTION via structured.check_stop_reason at runner.py:483, raising UnretryableOutputError; a text-mode injection bypasses check_stop_reason entirely and must NOT be used for this case.'

findings.finding: The 'What' section describes specific gate-level outcomes — 'plan-review systemic → INDETERMINATE + claim blocked; completion → fail-closed; code-review' — as explicit deliverables, but no AC specifically requires those outcome assertions to be present and correct. AC4 ('Two-scope (systemic vs per-criterion) and force/disabled guard scenarios are covered') verifies scenario coverage but not the enumerated gate outcomes, leaving the described work partially uncovered.
findings.location: What section (gate end-to-end bullet) vs Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Expand AC4 to: 'Two-scope (systemic vs per-criterion) scenarios are covered AND each gate-level outcome (plan-review systemic → INDETERMINATE + claim blocked; completion → fail-closed) is explicitly asserted per the What section enumeration; force/disabled guard scenarios are also covered.'

findings.finding: AC4 states 'force/disabled guard scenarios are covered' but specifies only that the guard 'never enters the handling path' — it does not state what the test asserts (e.g. no exception raised, specific return value, early return code) making the criterion unevaluable without inference.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Guard tests: --force / --force-close / feature-disabled never enters the handling path' and AC4: 'Two-scope (systemic vs per-criterion) and force/disabled guard scenarios are covered'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the hung-tool 'short outer test-level guard' (e.g., `pytest.mark.timeout(N)` + a tool that blocks indefinitely via `threading.Event`) and define what observable signal proves 'unbounded today' (e.g., the test harness timeout fires BEFORE any runner-level exception is raised).
findings.finding: The hung-tool scenario claims to assert 'UNbounded today' behavior but defers to 'a short outer test-level guard' without specifying the mechanism. Without a concrete timeout/thread/subprocess kill approach, the RED test cannot definitively prove the assertion (it could trivially pass by timing out before the tool call even starts, or yield a flaky result), and the plan provides no guidance on how to distinguish 'actually unbounded' from 'bounded by our test harness timeout'.
findings.location: Scope section — liveness scenarios paragraph: 'the hung-tool scenario pins that a stalled tool is UNbounded today (asserted via a short outer test-level guard, since the RED test cannot wait 600s)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a `threading.Event`-blocking tool function with `pytest-timeout` (e.g., 2s limit). Assert that the test's timeout fires rather than any rebar exception — that IS the proof of 'unbounded'. Document the signal explicitly in an inline comment: `# CURRENT(runner.py:285): hung tool has no per-tool timeout; test-level guard fires first`.

findings.finding: The What section names 'code-review' as a gate end-to-end scope to pin current behavior, but the plan never specifies WHAT the code-review gate's current behavior IS for any failure class. Plan-review behavior is described ('systemic → INDETERMINATE + claim blocked') and completion behavior is described ('fail-closed'). For code-review, the decision rule for each failure class is absent — an executor cannot write pinning assertions without independently investigating `gate_dispatch.py`.
findings.location: What section: 'gate end-to-end (plan-review systemic → INDETERMINATE + claim blocked; completion → fail-closed; code-review)'

findings.finding: The code-review gate's CURRENT behavior for each failure class is never described in the plan, even though 'code-review' is named as a gate end-to-end scope in the What section. Unlike plan-review (INDETERMINATE + claim blocked) and completion (fail-closed via LLMError raise), an executor has no decision rule to write the pinning assertion: code-review uses `_degraded_code_review_verdict` (returns `{verdict: INDETERMINATE, ...}` as a dict, never raises), but this is structurally distinct from the completion gate's `raise LLMError(...)` path, meaning assertions for all three gates require different patterns that only one (plan-review) is documented.
findings.location: What section — 'gate end-to-end (plan-review systemic → INDETERMINATE + claim blocked; completion → fail-closed; code-review)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'code-review gate: `produce_code_review_verdict` catches `LLMUnavailableError` both at preflight and mid-run, returning `{verdict: INDETERMINATE, coverage: {llm_unavailable: True}}` — never raises; assert `result["verdict"] == "INDETERMINATE"` for all systemic failure classes.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC2 (or the Scope section) how the meta-test enumerates expected parametrized ids — e.g., 'parse `@pytest.mark.parametrize` via `ast` on the same file' or 'grep the source for `# CASE:` sentinel comments that must match `# CURRENT(` comments' — so the executor has a concrete mechanism to implement.
findings.finding: The meta-test AC defers to the executor the design choice of how to enumerate 'each parametrized id' when checking that every parametrized case has a `# CURRENT(` comment — the plan does not specify whether the meta-test parses the AST, inspects pytest collection, or maintains a parallel explicit list.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'a meta-test asserts a # CURRENT( marker exists for each parametrized id'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC1 with the Scope description of the hung-tool scenario: either narrow AC1 to 'exercises every LLM-model-layer failure class via model_override' and add a separate AC covering the hung-tool liveness guard, or explicitly note in AC1 that the hung-tool case uses a test-level timeout guard rather than model_override.
findings.finding: AC1 says the test file 'exercises every failure class via `model_override`', but the Scope section describes the hung-tool liveness scenario as asserted 'via a short outer test-level guard, since the RED test cannot wait 600s' — a mechanism explicitly distinct from model_override injection. This creates a cross-section contradiction: the AC implies model_override is the universal injection seam, but at least one named scenario uses a different mechanism.
findings.location: AC item 1 vs Scope section ('Named liveness scenarios')
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC1 into two: (a) 'A parametrized test file exercises every LLM-failure-class via model_override, offline, guarded by ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS=False' and (b) 'The hung-tool liveness scenario is covered by a short outer test-level timeout guard (not model_override), asserting UNbounded behavior today.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the meta-test's enumeration protocol: define how it discovers the set of parametrized ids to check (e.g., 'maintain a MODULE-LEVEL `_FAILURE_CASE_IDS: list[str]` constant that is both the `pytest.mark.parametrize` id list and the meta-test's reference set; the meta-test iterates this constant and asserts each id appears in a `# CURRENT(` comment in the file').
findings.finding: The meta-test's enumeration source for parametrized ids is undefined: the plan requires 'a meta-test asserts a # CURRENT( marker exists for each parametrized id' but never specifies how the meta-test discovers the canonical set of parametrized ids to check against — leaving open whether it AST-parses the file, uses pytest's collection machinery, or maintains a static manifest list. Without this protocol the meta-test could pass vacuously (e.g., comparing an empty collected set against found markers, yielding 0 == 0).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, second bullet: 'a meta-test asserts a # CURRENT( marker exists for each parametrized id'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC bullet 2: 'The meta-test reads a module-level `_FAILURE_CASE_IDS` tuple (the single source of truth for case names), iterates each id, and asserts that the file source contains `# CURRENT(` followed by text referencing that id — this makes the meta-test non-vacuous and keeps the parametrize decorator and the coverage check in sync.'

findings.finding: The plan's failure matrix lists 'content-filter finish_reason' and 'a structured content-filter refusal' as two separate entries and says both raise `pydantic_ai.exceptions.ContentFilterError`/`IncompleteToolCall`, but their CURRENT runner-level error types differ in a load-bearing way: (a) a canned `finish_reason='content_filter'` response hits `structured.check_stop_reason()` inside `_pai_structured` (runner.py:483) → raises `UnretryableOutputError` (an `LLMRunnerError` subclass, caught by `except LLMError: raise` at line 351-352, NOT re-wrapped); (b) a FunctionModel that raises `ContentFilterError` propagates to `except Exception` at line 353 → wrapped to `LLMUnavailableError`. An implementer reading only the Scope bullet ('the test imports them at runtime') could assert the wrong error type for one of these two cases, making the pin silently incorrect.
findings.location: Scope section — 'The structured content-filter and incomplete-tool-call cases raise the corresponding `pydantic_ai.exceptions` types (`ContentFilterError` / `IncompleteToolCall`)'; runner.py:351-365 and runner.py:483-490
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope/matrix description, annotate each case with its expected runner-level exception: `finish_reason='content_filter'` → `pytest.raises(UnretryableOutputError)` (LLMRunnerError branch); raised `pydantic_ai.exceptions.ContentFilterError` → `pytest.raises(LLMUnavailableError)` (provider-failure branch). The `# CURRENT(runner.py:483)` and `# CURRENT(runner.py:353)` citations in the test file will then unambiguously point to the correct seam for each.

findings.finding: The per-failure-class routing table — which failure classes collapse to `LLMUnavailableError` vs `LLMRunnerError` vs `StructuredOutputError`/`UnretryableOutputError` at the runner seam — is never enumerated in the plan. The What section gives three broad buckets, but 'content_filter finish_reason', 'length/truncation', 'validation-retry exhaustion', and 'unparseable output' all currently surface as `UnretryableOutputError` or `StructuredOutputError` (subclasses of `LLMRunnerError`, not `LLMUnavailableError`). These are not provider failures and do not go through the `except Exception` wrap at runner.py:353. An executor relying on 'provider failures wrapped to LLMUnavailableError' as their classification rule will write wrong assertions for these four failure classes.
findings.location: What section — 'provider failures wrapped to LLMUnavailableError at the except seam, the step-budget path as LLMRunnerError, structured-output failures as StructuredOutputError'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default timeout mechanism and duration for the hung-tool guard (e.g., 'use `pytest-timeout` with a 5 s marker, or `asyncio.wait_for(..., timeout=2.0)`) so an executor can act without choosing.
findings.finding: The hung-tool scenario's 'short outer test-level guard' is a defaultable gap: the plan states the scenario must be asserted 'via a short outer test-level guard, since the RED test cannot wait 600s' but does not specify the mechanism (pytest-timeout plugin, asyncio.wait_for, threading.Timer, or a fixed sleep+check) or the guard duration.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Named liveness scenarios' / hung-tool scenario description
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the hung-tool scenario description: 'assert via `pytest.mark.timeout(5)` (pytest-timeout) or `asyncio.wait_for(..., timeout=2.0)`; the guard duration is 2–5 s.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the hung-tool test guard mechanism concretely (e.g., 'inject a FunctionModel whose tool-call handler sleeps indefinitely; wrap the runner call in a `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` with `timeout=<N>` seconds and assert `TimeoutError` is raised, confirming the runner provides no bounded tool timeout today').
findings.finding: The 'short outer test-level guard' mechanism for the hung-tool scenario is unspecified: the plan asserts 'a stalled tool is UNbounded today (asserted via a short outer test-level guard, since the RED test cannot wait 600s)' but names no concrete mechanism — no timeout value, no exception type expected, no threading/signal/subprocess technique. An executor cannot implement this without guessing the guard mechanism.
findings.location: Scope section, 'Named liveness scenarios pin CURRENT (pre-change) behavior only... the hung-tool scenario pins that a stalled tool is UNbounded today (asserted via a short outer test-level guard)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit CREATE/RETIRE lifecycle for `ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False` — e.g., an autouse session- or module-scoped pytest fixture that sets the flag in setup and unconditionally restores the original value in teardown (via `yield` + `finally`), so crash-in-test cannot bleed the flag into other modules.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False` as a guard but does not define the lifecycle (CREATE/RETIRE) for this global mutable flag: who sets it, when it is restored, and how teardown is guaranteed even on test failure.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – first bullet; Scope section ('Guarded by `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False`')
findings.suggested_fix:
Add an autouse fixture at module or session scope:
```python
import pytest
import pydantic_ai.models as _pai_models

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True, scope='module')
def _block_real_model_requests():
    original = _pai_models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS
    _pai_models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False
    yield
    _pai_models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = original
```
This guarantees teardown even on test failure and is self-documenting in the plan's AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract `_function_model` / `_sequence_model` (and the new raising/canned variants) into a shared `tests/unit/conftest.py` fixture or `tests/unit/_llm_test_helpers.py` module rather than creating a third private copy in the new file (Rule-of-Three: 2 existing copies already exist in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:26-52` and `test_runner_hardening.py:23-36`).
findings.finding: The plan implicitly introduces a third independent in-file copy of the `_function_model` / `_sequence_model` FunctionModel factory helpers. Identical helpers already exist in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:26-52` (6 call-sites) and `test_runner_hardening.py:23-36` (3 call-sites). The new file's raising/canned-response injection wrappers are the same pattern. Three independent copies crosses the Rule-of-Three threshold and is the very duplication the rule guards against.
findings.location: What section / AC #1 (new `tests/unit/test_llm_failure_matrix.py`)
findings.suggested_fix: Create `tests/unit/_llm_test_helpers.py` exporting `function_model(json_out)`, `sequence_model(texts)`, and (new) `raising_model(exc)` / `canned_finish_reason_model(reason)`. Import from there in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py`, `test_runner_hardening.py`, and the new `test_llm_failure_matrix.py`. This is a one-time extraction that pays off immediately across 3 files and all future injection-based tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `# CURRENT(` meta-test is worth its maintenance cost given it is a novel single-file abstraction with no existing precedent (zero grep hits); consider replacing it with a simpler doc-convention (e.g. a module-level docstring table) that achieves the same cross-story navigation goal without a self-referential comment-scanner test.
findings.finding: AC #2 mandates a bespoke meta-test that scans source comments for `# CURRENT(` markers to enforce per-parametrized-id citation coverage. This is a novel comment-presence enforcement abstraction with zero existing call-sites or precedent in the test suite. It serves only this one new file and is not reused elsewhere — failing the Rule-of-Three bar for a new abstraction.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #2: 'a meta-test asserts a `# CURRENT(` marker exists for each parametrized id'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the existing `_function_model`/`_sequence_model` helpers in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:26-52` and `test_runner_hardening.py:23-36` as related consumers; either import from one shared location or explicitly note why a third in-file copy is preferred (e.g. the new file is intentionally self-contained for the baseline role).
findings.finding: The plan will introduce a third independent copy of the `_function_model` / `_sequence_model` FunctionModel factory helpers in the new test file, but identical helpers already exist in `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:26-52` and `tests/unit/test_runner_hardening.py:23-36`. These existing helpers are consumers of the same pydantic-ai seam the new file needs, but the plan does not enumerate them or direct the new file to import from a shared location — leaving three diverging in-file copies.
findings.location: Scope section / implied new file `tests/unit/test_llm_failure_matrix.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Extract `_function_model` / `_sequence_model` into a shared `tests/unit/conftest.py` or `tests/unit/fixtures/llm_helpers.py` and import from there in all three test files, OR add a note in the plan explicitly accepting the in-file copy and its maintenance implications.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the experiment log with a [7] entry that explicitly injects `ModelResponse(finish_reason='content_filter')` via FunctionModel and confirms it is accepted without error and readable at `result.response.finish_reason`, resolving whether 'content_filter' is a valid/accepted finish_reason string in pydantic-ai 1.107.0.
findings.finding: The experiment log verifies `finish_reason='length'` is readable ([3]), but never verifies that `finish_reason='content_filter'` is an accepted canned value that a FunctionModel can return without pydantic-ai raising a validation error. If pydantic-ai enumerates or validates finish_reason values, returning an unlisted string could raise an unexpected exception inside the model layer, silently converting the canned-finish_reason test case into an exception-propagation test case — the opposite of its intended mechanism.
findings.location: Scope section — 'content_filter finish_reason case' bullet; Verified library behavior experiment log [3]
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the experiment script: construct a FunctionModel that returns ModelResponse([TextPart('')], finish_reason='content_filter'), run the agent, and assert result.response.finish_reason == 'content_filter' with no exception. If pydantic-ai uses a FinishReason enum/Literal, the correct value (e.g. FinishReason.content_filter) should be used in the test instead of the raw string.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the experiment log (or the plan's Scope) the exact constructor call or post-construction assignment form used to inject `finish_reason` into a `ModelResponse` for offline FunctionModel injection — e.g. confirm whether it's `ModelResponse(parts=[...], finish_reason='content_filter')`, a dataclass field assignment after construction, or a different mechanism.
findings.finding: The `content_filter` finish_reason injection case relies on `ModelResponse(finish_reason='content_filter')` as a constructor form, but every existing codebase usage of ModelResponse constructs it with only `parts=[...]` as a keyword argument, and experiment log item [3] only proves `result.response.finish_reason` is *readable* — not that `ModelResponse` accepts `finish_reason` as a constructor keyword. If `finish_reason` is a post-construction-only field or is set through a different mechanism (e.g. via a `ModelResponsePart` or a provider-specific field), the injection will fail at collection time.
findings.location: Scope section — 'content_filter finish_reason case' bullet; experiment log item [3]
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the experiment script to include: `from pydantic_ai.messages import ModelResponse, TextPart; r = ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart('x')], finish_reason='content_filter'); print(r.finish_reason)` and record the output; then cite that specific form in the Scope's finish_reason bullet.

findings.finding: The plan states 'structured-output failures as `StructuredOutputError`' as the pinned current type for the content_filter finish_reason and similar cases, but the actual raised type is `UnretryableOutputError` (a subclass of `StructuredOutputError`) per structured.py:243 and the `_UNRETRYABLE_STOP_REASONS` frozenset. A `# CURRENT` comment or `pytest.raises(StructuredOutputError)` assertion passes whether the runner raises `StructuredOutputError` or `UnretryableOutputError`, so the regression net fails to detect a change from `UnretryableOutputError` to plain `StructuredOutputError` (a real regression — it would re-enable the retry loop on content_filter). The plan's 'pin CURRENT behavior' goal is defeated at this level of precision.
findings.location: What section — 'structured-output failures as `StructuredOutputError`'; Scope — content_filter finish_reason case
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'structured-output failures as `StructuredOutputError`' with: 'structured-output failures: `UnretryableOutputError` for content_filter/length/refusal/max_tokens finish_reasons (fast-fail path, structured.py:243), plain `StructuredOutputError` for parse/validation failures (structured.py:157-199) — both are `LLMRunnerError` subclasses, but the subtype distinction IS the regression guard.'

findings.finding: The plan's runner-level error classification for the `content_filter` finish_reason and `length`/truncation cases is wrong: the plan says these surface as `StructuredOutputError`, but structured.py:242-243 shows `check_stop_reason` raises `UnretryableOutputError` (a StructuredOutputError subclass) for those stop reasons. The `# CURRENT()` comments and `pytest.raises()` assertions must pin `UnretryableOutputError`, not plain `StructuredOutputError`, or they will silently over-catch.
findings.location: Scope section / What section: 'structured-output failures as StructuredOutputError'; structured.py:242-243
findings.suggested_fix: In the failure matrix table and in the test parametrize list, annotate `content_filter` (finish_reason path) and `length`/`max_tokens` as raising `UnretryableOutputError` (imported from `rebar.llm.errors`). The `# CURRENT(src/rebar/llm/structured.py:242)` comment should read: 'check_stop_reason raises UnretryableOutputError for unretryable stop reasons'.

findings.finding: The plan's What section states 'structured-output failures as `StructuredOutputError`' as the current runner-level type for the content_filter finish_reason and length/truncation cases, but `structured.check_stop_reason` (called at `runner.py:483`) raises `UnretryableOutputError` — a distinct subclass of `StructuredOutputError` — for those exact stop reasons (`content_filter`, `length`, `refusal`, `max_tokens`). A test asserting `pytest.raises(StructuredOutputError)` will pass vacuously for both, but one asserting the ACTUAL current type needs `UnretryableOutputError`. The plan never names `UnretryableOutputError` as a pinned type in the routing table.
findings.location: What section: 'structured-output failures as StructuredOutputError'; Scope: 'content_filter finish_reason', 'length/truncation'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section's routing table, change 'structured-output failures as `StructuredOutputError`' to distinguish: (a) UnretryableOutputError for content_filter/length/refusal/max_tokens finish_reason (fast-fail path, runner.py:486); (b) plain StructuredOutputError for unparseable output and validation-retry exhaustion (runner.py:491+499). Both are LLMRunnerError subclasses.

findings.finding: The plan injects the 'content_filter finish_reason' failure class via `ModelResponse(finish_reason="content_filter")`, but every existing codebase usage of ModelResponse uses only `ModelResponse(parts=[...])`. The experiment log entry [3] only verifies READING `result.response.finish_reason` off a completed run result — it does NOT verify that ModelResponse accepts `finish_reason` as a constructor argument. If the pydantic-ai ModelResponse constructor does not accept `finish_reason` as a direct kwarg (it may be a property derived from the parts, or require a specific FinishReason literal type), the content_filter finish_reason injection case would fail at test collection or produce an unexpected result — silently invalidating a critical failure-matrix case.
findings.location: Scope section — 'content_filter finish_reason' bullet; experiment log entry [3]

findings.finding: The plan's 'Guard tests: `--force` / `--force-close` / feature-disabled never enters the handling path' conflates two unrelated concepts. `--force` and `--force-close` are CLI ticket-close flags (`_commands/transition.py`, `_commands/transition_close.py`) that have no presence in the LLM runner or gate_dispatch.py. There is no `--force` guard in the LLM failure-handling path. The actual LLM-layer 'feature disabled' guard is `enabled=False` / `code_review_enabled()` returning False in gate_dispatch.py:499, which short-circuits to `_inert_code_review_verdict()` with zero LLM calls.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Guard tests: `--force` / `--force-close` / feature-disabled never enters the handling path'

findings.finding: The plan treats 'content_filter finish_reason' and 'a structured content-filter refusal' as two separate failure matrix entries injected by 'raising `ContentFilterError`' (the pydantic-ai exception class). But in the actual runner, the `content_filter` finish_reason is handled by `structured.check_stop_reason()` → rebar's `UnretryableOutputError` (a `StructuredOutputError` subclass), NOT by `ContentFilterError` being raised from the model layer. `ContentFilterError` (from pydantic-ai) would be caught by the runner's bare `except Exception` → `LLMUnavailableError`, while `check_stop_reason("content_filter")` → `UnretryableOutputError` is caught by `except LLMError: raise`. These are distinct runner-level behaviors, and the plan conflates their injection mechanisms.
findings.location: Scope section: 'content-filter finish_reason, a structured content-filter refusal' failure matrix entries; What section: 'structured-output failures as `StructuredOutputError`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief section to CLAUDE.md (or an inline README near `tests/unit/`) documenting the `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>)` convention: what it means, how later stories use it to flip RED→GREEN, and how to add a new failure class.
findings.finding: The `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>)` inline comment convention — a new cross-story protocol that later stories rely on to identify and flip pinned behaviors — is not documented in any persistent artifact (CLAUDE.md, README, or ADR). A new contributor sees the meta-test enforcement but not the architectural rationale (why this convention exists, what 'flip to GREEN' means, or how to add a new failure class in a later story).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (bullet 2) and Scope ('the named baseline that later stories flip to the new behavior')
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to CLAUDE.md under a 'Testing conventions' heading:
```
## LLM Failure Baseline (`tests/unit/test_llm_failure_matrix.py`)
Each parametrized case carries a `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>): <behavior>` comment citing the runner/gate seam it pins. A meta-test asserts every case has this marker. When a later story changes that behavior, update the assertion AND the CURRENT comment to reflect the new seam. To add a new failure class: add a parametrize entry, inject via model_override, assert observed behavior, add the CURRENT comment.
```

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
